paper.title: Is One Layer Enough? Training A Single Transformer Layer Can Match Full-Parameter RL Training
paper.summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a central component of post-training large language models (LLMs), yet little is understood about how RL adaptation is distributed across transformer layers. Existing approaches typically update all model parameters uniformly, implicitly assuming that every layer contributes similarly to the gains obtained during RL post-training. The authors challenge this assumption through a systematic layer-wise study of RL training and find that training a single transformer layer can recover most of the gains achieved by full-parameter RL training, and in some cases even surpass it. To quantify this phenomenon they introduce the 'layer contribution' metric, C(k). Across seven models spanning two model families (Qwen3, Qwen2.5), three RL algorithms (GRPO, GiGPO, Dr. GRPO), and multiple task domains, RL gains are highly concentrated in a small subset of, and in many cases even a single, transformer layer -- typically located in the middle of the stack.

source.raw_context: This procedure is repeated independently for every layer k in {0,...,L-1}, isolating each layer's ability to absorb RL-induced improvement. Each resulting model is then evaluated on the same set of in-domain benchmarks to obtain a performance score. To quantify each layer's capacity to capture RL-induced improvement, we define layer contribution. Let S_k denote the in-domain performance of the model trained on layer k, measured as the average score across in-domain benchmarks. Let S_base denote the performance of the original pretrained model without any RL training, and S_full denote the performance of the model after standard full-parameter GRPO training. The layer contribution of layer k is: C(k) = (S_k - S_base) / (S_full - S_base). A layer contribution of 1.0 indicates that single-layer training fully matches the gain of full-parameter training; values above 1.0 indicate that it surpasses full-parameter training; values near 0 indicate that the layer fails to capture meaningful RL improvements.

symbol_map.meaning: The layer contribution of layer k: the fraction of full-parameter RL's improvement that is recovered by training only layer k in isolation (all other parameters frozen).
symbol_map.grounding_sentence: To quantify each layer's capacity to capture RL-induced improvement, we define layer contribution... The layer contribution of layer k is: C(k) = (S_k - S_base) / (S_full - S_base).

symbol_map.meaning: Index of a single transformer layer, ranging over all L layers of the model (k in {0, ..., L-1}); this is the layer that was selectively unfrozen and trained with GRPO while all other parameters stayed fixed.
symbol_map.grounding_sentence: This procedure is repeated independently for every layer k in {0,...,L-1}, isolating each layer's ability to absorb RL-induced improvement.

symbol_map.meaning: In-domain benchmark performance (average score) of the model after training only layer k with RL, all other layers frozen.
symbol_map.grounding_sentence: Let S_k denote the in-domain performance of the model trained on layer k, measured as the average score across in-domain benchmarks.

symbol_map.meaning: Baseline performance of the original pretrained model before any RL training is applied.
symbol_map.grounding_sentence: Let S_base denote the performance of the original pretrained model without any RL training.

symbol_map.meaning: Performance of the model after standard full-parameter GRPO training (every layer updated), the gold-standard upper reference the single layer is compared against.
symbol_map.grounding_sentence: S_full denote the performance of the model after standard full-parameter GRPO training.

color_spans.label: Layer contribution
color_spans.explanation: The score this equation defines: how much of the full RL improvement one isolated layer k can capture on its own, expressed as a fraction/ratio.

color_spans.label: Single-layer gain (numerator)
color_spans.explanation: The improvement that training only layer k buys you: its benchmark score S_k minus the untrained baseline score S_base. This isolates the RL-induced gain from training just one layer.

color_spans.label: Full-parameter RL gain (denominator)
color_spans.explanation: The total improvement achieved when every layer in the model is updated by standard full-parameter RL -- the normalizing reference. Dividing by this turns the single-layer gain into a fraction of the best-case full-model gain.

plain_english_explanation: This equation defines a single number, C(k), that tells you what percentage of the total benefit of RL fine-tuning you get if you only unfreeze and train one transformer layer, layer k, instead of the whole model. The numerator, S_k minus S_base, is the raw improvement that single-layer training buys you over the untrained baseline. The denominator, S_full minus S_base, is the raw improvement that ordinary full-parameter RL training buys you over that same baseline. Dividing the two normalizes single-layer training against the best case (updating everything), so C(k) = 1.0 means training just layer k recovers all of full-parameter RL's benefit, values above 1.0 mean it does even better, and values near 0 mean that layer barely helps at all.
intuitive_analogy: Imagine a car's overall speedup after a full engine tune-up (S_full versus its stock performance S_base). Now imagine only replacing one component -- say, just the spark plugs -- and measuring the speedup that gives (S_k versus S_base). Layer contribution C(k) is the ratio of the single-part upgrade's speedup to the full tune-up's speedup: if replacing just the spark plugs gets you 90% of the full tune-up's benefit, that part had a layer contribution of 0.9, telling you it was doing most of the real work all along.

callout_boxes.text: This is the paper's headline quantity: it operationalizes 'how much does this one layer matter for RL gains' as a single normalized ratio, enabling direct comparison across 7 models, 3 RL algorithms, and multiple tasks.

S_k comes from repeating the single-layer training procedure (Eq. 3 in the paper) independently for every layer k, then evaluating that resulting model on in-domain benchmarks. Subtracting S_base isolates the marginal RL-induced gain rather than raw model quality.
This denominator is fixed per model/task/algorithm -- it's computed once from standard full-parameter GRPO training and then reused to normalize every layer's C(k).

source_quotes.quote: To quantify each layer's capacity to capture RL-induced improvement, we define layer contribution.
Let S_k denote the in-domain performance of the model trained on layer k, measured as the average score across in-domain benchmarks.
Let S_base denote the performance of the original pretrained model without any RL training, and S_full denote the performance of the model after standard full-parameter GRPO training.

A layer contribution of 1.0 indicates that single-layer training fully matches the gain of full-parameter training; values above 1.0 indicate that it surpasses full-parameter training; values near 0 indicate that the layer fails to capture meaningful RL improvements.
Surprisingly, we find that training a single transformer layer can recover most of the gains achieved by full-parameter RL training, and in some cases even surpass it.

confidence.notes: All symbols and the equation itself were read verbatim from the arXiv HTML (LaTeXML alttext), which is the authoritative equation source. The surrounding prose gives explicit, unambiguous definitions for every symbol (S_k, S_base, S_full, k, C(k)) directly adjacent to the equation, so grounding is strong and not inferential. The only minor softness: 'in-domain benchmarks' and the exact benchmark list are described a few paragraphs later in the paper rather than inline with the equation; confidence would reach ~1.0 by also quoting that benchmark list verbatim.
